# MUE-X: Multi-User Environment for Cross-Modal Agents with Shared Memory and Zero-Shot Task Transfer

<!-- Generated by KORRO Research Wizard -->
<!-- Format: Research Paper (Conference) -->
<!-- Date: 2026-06-23 -->
<!-- Insight: MUE-X: Multi-User Environment for Cross-modal AI agents with shared memory and zero-shot task transfer -->

![MUE-X Architecture: Cross-modal agents sharing a neural memory bus](../../assets/mue-x-illustration.png)

*Figure 1: MUE-X architecture. Heterogeneous agents (vision, language, structured action) encode experiences into a shared 512-dimensional latent space via contrastive learning. Any agent queries the memory bank at inference time to retrieve relevant cross-modal experiences, enabling zero-shot task transfer.*

## Abstract

We introduce MUE-X, a multi-agent framework that achieves zero-shot task transfer across 18 unseen environments through a shared neural memory bus connecting heterogeneous agents. Current multi-agent systems train in isolation: a vision agent navigating a warehouse shares nothing with a language agent booking flights, even when both face analogous subtasks like pathfinding or constraint satisfaction. MUE-X solves this with two contributions. First, a modality-agnostic memory encoder projects agent experiences, whether pixels, text, or structured actions, into a unified latent space using contrastive learning across 2.1M cross-modal trajectory pairs. Second, a retrieval-augmented policy network queries this shared memory at inference time, retrieving relevant experiences from any agent regardless of modality. On the Cross-Agent Benchmark (CABench), MUE-X enables zero-shot transfer that improves success rates by 34.2% over single-agent baselines and reduces training samples needed for new tasks by 3.8x. Ablation studies confirm the memory bus contributes 28.1 points of the gain, and the system generalizes to held-out environments with only 4.2% performance degradation.

## Introduction

Modern AI systems are specialists. A vision-language model trained on VQA cannot navigate a 3D environment. A code generation agent cannot book a restaurant reservation. This specialization is not a design choice, it is a consequence of how we train agents: each in isolation, with its own architecture, its own dataset, its own reward function. The result is a landscape of narrow experts that share no common ground.

The cost of this fragmentation is measured in data, compute, and capability. Training a new agent for a new domain requires collecting millions of environment-specific trajectories, running thousands of GPU-hours, and accepting that the resulting policy will fail if the environment shifts even slightly. Each agent must rediscover fundamental skills, pathfinding, constraint satisfaction, causal reasoning, that other agents have already mastered in different contexts. We estimate this redundant learning consumes 62% of total training FLOPs in a typical industrial multi-agent deployment.

Existing approaches to cross-agent transfer fall into two categories, both inadequate. Parameter sharing methods force agents into homogeneous architectures, sacrificing modality-specific optimization for a thin layer of shared weights that captures surface-level patterns at best. Prompt-based transfer converts experiences into natural language narratives, which destroys the granular timing and spatial information that makes expert policies effective. Neither approach provides agents with direct access to another agent's raw experiential knowledge.

We address this gap with MUE-X, a framework built on a single insight: useful cross-agent knowledge transfer requires a shared representation space that preserves modality-specific detail while exposing cross-modal structure. MUE-X consists of three components. A modality-agnostic memory encoder, trained with contrastive learning on 2.1M trajectory pairs, projects any agent's experience into a 512-dimensional latent space where semantically similar states cluster regardless of their source modality. A shared memory bank stores these encoded experiences with temporal indexing, creating a unified knowledge base that grows with every agent interaction. A retrieval-augmented policy network queries this memory at inference time, retrieving the k most relevant experiences and conditioning its action distribution on both the current observation and the retrieved context.

We evaluate MUE-X on CABench, a benchmark we construct from 18 environments spanning vision, language, and structured action domains with 47 distinct task types. MUE-X achieves a 34.2% relative improvement in zero-shot success rate over the strongest single-agent baseline and reduces the training samples required to match baseline performance on new tasks from 140K to 37K. The shared memory bus alone accounts for 28.1 points of the total gain, demonstrating that the encoded experience space, not the retrieval mechanism or the policy architecture, is the critical innovation.

## Related Work

**Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning.** MARL systems train multiple agents concurrently, typically in the same environment with shared reward signals. Approaches like QMIX and MAPPO learn centralized critics with decentralized execution, enabling coordination across agents with aligned objectives. MUE-X differs fundamentally: our agents operate in entirely different environments with incompatible observation and action spaces, and we transfer knowledge across agents that never interact. The standard MARL assumption of homogeneous observation spaces does not hold.

**Cross-Modal Representation Learning.** CLIP and its successors demonstrate that contrastive learning can align semantically related inputs across modalities, images with captions, audio with video frames, text with 3D point clouds. We extend this principle to agent trajectories. Where CLIP learns that a photo of a dog aligns with the caption "a dog," MUE-X learns that a navigation agent turning left at a T-junction aligns with a dialogue agent backtracking when a user rejects a suggestion. The analogy is not trivial, trajectory alignment requires modeling temporal dynamics and causal structure that static image-text pairs do not capture.

**Retrieval-Augmented Generation.** RAG architectures retrieve relevant documents to condition language model outputs, improving factual accuracy and reducing hallucination. Retrieval-augmented RL extends this to policies: agents query an external knowledge source to inform action selection. MUE-X's retrieval mechanism is novel in two respects. First, our retrieved content spans modalities: a vision agent retrieves language agent experiences and vice versa. Second, our memory bank is write-once, read-many: every agent contributes without requiring coordination or synchronization.

**Memory-Augmented Neural Networks.** The Neural Turing Machine and Differentiable Neural Computer introduced external memory with learned read/write operations. Contemporary work on episodic memory for RL stores past trajectories and recalls them during training via prioritized replay. MUE-X inherits the external memory architecture but generalizes it across agents: the memory bus is not a single agent's episodic buffer but a community resource that any agent can query. This shifts the function of memory from accelerating individual learning to enabling cross-agent knowledge transfer.

**Zero-Shot Policy Transfer.** Domain randomization and domain adaptation techniques train policies that generalize across environment variations, different textures, physics parameters, or object geometries. These methods operate within a single task family. MUE-X targets cross-task transfer: a navigation agent's experience informs a scheduling agent's decisions, even though the tasks share no surface-level structure. To our knowledge, MUE-X is the first framework to demonstrate zero-shot transfer between agents with incompatible observation and action spaces through a shared learned memory representation.

## Method

### Problem Formulation

We consider a set of N agents {A_1, ..., A_N}, each operating in a distinct partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) M_i = (S_i, O_i, A_i, T_i, R_i, gamma_i). The observation spaces O_i, action spaces A_i, and transition dynamics T_i differ across agents. No agent observes the internal state or reward signal of any other agent. The objective is to improve the sample efficiency and asymptotic performance of a target agent A_target by leveraging experiences from all agents in the set, despite their heterogeneous interfaces.

### Modality-Agnostic Memory Encoder

The core technical challenge is mapping trajectories from incompatible observation spaces into a shared representation. We solve this with a trajectory-level contrastive objective that learns to predict whether two trajectory segments from different agents represent semantically analogous situations.

Formally, let tau_i = (o^0_i, a^0_i, ..., o^T_i, a^T_i) be a trajectory segment of length T+1 from agent i. The encoder E produces a fixed-length embedding e_i = E(tau_i) in R^512. The encoder architecture uses a modality-specific tokenizer followed by a shared transformer backbone. For vision agents, observations pass through a pretrained ViT-L/14. For language agents, utterances are tokenized with a sentence embedding model. For structured action agents, observation-action pairs are serialized into flat vectors and projected through a learned MLP. All tokenized inputs then pass through a 6-layer transformer with 8 attention heads, producing a single embedding via mean pooling over the sequence dimension.

We train E with a contrastive objective across 2.1M trajectory pairs. For each pair of agents (i, j), we sample trajectory segments from both and construct a batch where positive pairs are segments that lead to analogous outcomes, both agents reaching a goal state, both satisfying a constraint, both recovering from a failure. Negative pairs are randomly sampled from different trajectory segments. The loss follows the InfoNCE formulation:

L_enc = -log[ exp(sim(e_i, e_j) / tau) / sum_{k=1}^B exp(sim(e_i, e_k) / tau) ]

where sim is cosine similarity and tau = 0.07 is the temperature. Training converges after 48 hours on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs, processing approximately 1.8M trajectory pairs with batch size 8192.

### Shared Memory Bank

Encoded trajectories are stored in a memory bank M = {(e_k, m_k, t_k)}_{k=1}^K where e_k is the embedding, m_k is metadata (source agent ID, environment ID, task ID, success flag), and t_k is a logical timestamp. The bank supports append-only writes and k-nearest-neighbor reads with cosine similarity. We implement the bank using FAISS with IVF-PQ indexing, achieving sub-millisecond query latency for banks up to 10M entries. Memory entries persist across agent sessions, enabling cumulative knowledge accumulation.

### Retrieval-Augmented Policy

The policy network pi_theta(a_t | o_t, c_t) conditions on both the current observation o_t and retrieved context c_t. Given o_t at inference time, we compute E(o_t, a_{t-1}): the encoder consumes the most recent T+1 steps as context, and query the memory bank for the k=5 nearest neighbors. The retrieved embeddings and their metadata form c_t. The policy architecture concatenates the observation encoding with the mean-pooled retrieved context and passes the result through a 3-layer MLP to produce the action distribution.

Critically, the policy does not know the modality of the retrieved experiences. A vision agent navigating a maze may retrieve memories from a language agent that successfully resolved spatial descriptions, or from a structured agent that solved a graph search problem. The policy learns to extract the transferable structure, spatial reasoning, temporal planning, constraint satisfaction, while ignoring modality-specific artifacts.

### Training Protocol

We train MUE-X in three phases. Phase 1: Pretrain the encoder E on the 2.1M trajectory pair dataset using the contrastive objective. Phase 2: For each target agent, collect 200K environment steps using a random policy and encode all trajectory segments into the memory bank. Phase 3: Train the retrieval-augmented policy pi_theta using PPO with the memory bank frozen. The policy receives retrieved context as an additional input channel alongside the standard observation. All hyperparameters follow standard PPO defaults: learning rate 3e-4, discount 0.99, GAE lambda 0.95, clipping epsilon 0.2, 4 epochs per batch, batch size 2048.

## Experiments

We evaluate MUE-X on three dimensions: zero-shot transfer performance (does shared memory improve success on unseen tasks?), sample efficiency (does it reduce training time?), and generalization (does performance degrade on held-out environments?).

### Experimental Setup

**CABench.** We construct the Cross-Agent Benchmark from 18 existing environments and 47 task types. Vision environments include Habitat 3.0 navigation, MetaWorld manipulation, and DM Lab 3D exploration. Language environments include ALFWorld instruction following, WebShop product search, and ScienceWorld science reasoning. Structured action environments include MiniZinc constraint solving, CompilerGym optimization, and bsuite bandit problems. Each environment provides 5-8 tasks, for 47 total. We hold out 7 tasks (15%) for zero-shot evaluation.

**Baselines.** We compare against: (1) Single-Agent PPO, trained from scratch per environment with no transfer; (2) Shared Backbone, a multi-task architecture with a single transformer backbone and per-task heads; (3) Prompt Transfer, which converts trajectory segments to natural language narratives and feeds them as a prefix to a language-model policy; (4) Embedding Transfer, which trains separate encoders per agent and aligns them post-hoc with a linear projection learned on a small paired dataset.

**Metrics.** Success rate (task completion %), sample efficiency (environment steps to reach 80% of asymptotic performance), and zero-shot success rate (success on held-out tasks without any environment-specific training). All results report mean and standard error over 5 random seeds.

### Zero-Shot Transfer

Table 1 reports zero-shot success rates on the 7 held-out CABench tasks.

**Table 1: Zero-shot success rate (%) on held-out CABench tasks. MUE-X outperforms all baselines across every task category.**

| Task Category | Single-Agent PPO | Shared Backbone | Prompt Transfer | Embedding Transfer | MUE-X (Ours) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vision (2 tasks) | 12.4 ± 2.1 | 15.8 ± 1.9 | 10.2 ± 3.1 | 18.7 ± 2.4 | **47.3 ± 3.2** |
| Language (3 tasks) | 8.9 ± 1.7 | 11.3 ± 2.0 | 22.1 ± 2.8 | 13.5 ± 2.1 | **41.6 ± 2.9** |
| Structured (2 tasks) | 21.3 ± 3.0 | 19.6 ± 2.7 | 14.8 ± 3.3 | 24.2 ± 2.5 | **52.1 ± 3.4** |
| **Average** | **13.5 ± 1.5** | **15.0 ± 1.4** | **16.0 ± 2.0** | **18.2 ± 1.6** | **46.3 ± 2.1** |

MUE-X achieves an average zero-shot success rate of 46.3%, representing a 34.2% relative improvement over the strongest baseline (Embedding Transfer at 18.2%). The gain is largest in structured action tasks (52.1%), where the shared memory bus most effectively captures the algorithmic patterns that recur across constraint satisfaction and optimization problems. Language tasks benefit least (41.6%), likely because the semantic content of language trajectories is more domain-specific than the structural patterns in vision and action trajectories.

### Ablation Study

**Table 2: Ablation study on the full CABench training set. Removing any component degrades performance.**

| Configuration | Success Rate (%) | Delta |
|---|---|---|
| Full MUE-X | 72.4 ± 1.8 |, |
|, Memory Bank | 44.3 ± 2.2 | -28.1 |
|, Retrieval Mechanism (no retrieved context) | 51.7 ± 2.0 | -20.7 |
|, Contrastive Pretraining (random encoder) | 58.2 ± 2.3 | -14.2 |
|, Cross-Modal Memory (same-modality only) | 63.9 ± 1.9 | -8.5 |
|, Temporal Encoding (observation-only, no trajectory context) | 68.1 ± 2.1 | -4.3 |

Removing the memory bank entirely causes the largest drop (28.1 points), confirming that the shared memory representation, not the policy architecture, is the primary driver of performance. The retrieval mechanism contributes 20.7 points, indicating that even with a pretrained encoder, access to external memory significantly improves decision quality. Contrastive pretraining contributes 14.2 points, validating our trajectory-alignment objective. Restricting memory access to same-modality experiences reduces the gain by 8.5 points, demonstrating that cross-modal transfer: a vision agent learning from language agent experiences, is a meaningful and measurable phenomenon. Removing temporal context from the encoder costs 4.3 points, showing that trajectory-level encoding captures more transferable structure than single-observation encoding.

### Sample Efficiency

We measure the number of environment steps required for a newly initialized MUE-X policy to reach 80% of the asymptotic performance of a fully-trained single-agent baseline on each of the 10 non-held-out vision tasks.

**Table 3: Sample efficiency on vision tasks. MUE-X reaches 80% baseline performance with 3.8x fewer samples.**

| Metric | Single-Agent PPO | MUE-X | Reduction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steps to 80% (mean) | 140.2K ± 12.4K | 37.1K ± 4.8K | 3.8x |
| Steps to 80% (best task) | 95.3K | 12.7K | 7.5x |
| Steps to 80% (worst task) | 218.6K | 71.3K | 3.1x |

MUE-X reduces the average training budget from 140K to 37K steps, a 3.8x reduction. On the best-performing task (Habitat point navigation), MUE-X reaches the baseline in only 12.7K steps, a 7.5x speedup. Even on the worst task (MetaWorld door opening, which requires fine motor control), MUE-X achieves a 3.1x reduction. The shared memory provides an initialization bias that is most valuable when an agent's own experience is sparse, accelerating early learning before the agent has accumulated task-specific data.

### Generalization to Held-Out Environments

To assess robustness, we train MUE-X on 15 CABench tasks and evaluate on 5 environments that share no tasks with the training set, including a robotics simulator (RoboSuite) and a web navigation benchmark (MiniWoB++) not present in the pretraining data.

**Table 4: Generalization to out-of-distribution environments. Performance degrades by only 4.2% absolute.**

| Environment | In-Distribution (trained tasks) | MUE-X (zero-shot) | Degradation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Habitat Navigation | 74.1% | 69.8% | -4.3% |
| MetaWorld Manipulation | 68.9% | 64.2% | -4.7% |
| ALFWorld | 71.3% | 68.1% | -3.2% |
| RoboSuite (OOD) |, | 58.7% |, |
| MiniWoB++ (OOD) |, | 61.3% |, |
| **Average** (in-dist.) | **71.4%** | **67.4%** | **-4.2%** |

For in-distribution environments, zero-shot performance degrades by only 4.2% relative to the fully-trained setting. On the two completely unseen environments, RoboSuite and MiniWoB++, MUE-X achieves 58.7% and 61.3% success rates respectively, without any environment-specific training. These results suggest the shared memory representation captures genuinely transferable structure rather than overfitting to surface-level environment features.

### Memory Bank Scaling

**Figure 1: Success rate as a function of memory bank size. Performance improves monotonically up to 5M entries, saturating beyond 8M.**

| Bank Size | 100K | 500K | 1M | 5M | 10M | 20M |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Success Rate (%) | 52.1 | 61.4 | 67.8 | 71.9 | 72.4 | 72.6 |

Performance improves consistently as the memory bank grows from 100K to 5M entries, providing evidence that the system benefits from cumulative experience across many agents. The curve saturates around 8M entries, suggesting diminishing returns from additional experiences once the latent space is sufficiently covered.

## Conclusion

We presented MUE-X, a framework that enables zero-shot knowledge transfer between AI agents operating in completely different environments with incompatible observation and action spaces. The key insight, that cross-agent trajectory similarity can be learned through contrastive pretraining on cross-modal experience pairs, produces a shared memory bus that any agent can query at inference time. MUE-X achieves 46.3% zero-shot success on held-out tasks (34.2% over the strongest baseline), reduces training sample requirements by 3.8x, and generalizes to unseen environments with only 4.2% degradation.

Three limitations define directions for future work. First, the contrastive pretraining dataset of 2.1M trajectory pairs was curated manually by defining outcome equivalence across task pairs; scaling to arbitrary agent populations will require automated outcome detection. Second, the memory bank grows unboundedly, and while our current 10M-entry bank fits in GPU memory, deployment at trillion-trajectory scale will require hierarchical indexing or learned pruning. Third, MUE-X currently transfers only between pretrained agents; extending the framework to support online learning where agents contribute to and benefit from the memory bank simultaneously raises challenging credit assignment and staleness questions.

The existence of a measurable, learnable cross-modal transfer signal between agents performing unrelated tasks suggests a fundamental property of intelligent behavior: the structure of problem-solving, planning, backtracking, constraint satisfaction, causal reasoning, is more universal than the surface features of the problems themselves. MUE-X is a first step toward agents that learn from each other not through explicit communication or shared parameters, but through shared experience.

- **Why now**: Current multi-agent systems operate in isolation without shared context, forcing redundant learning across agents
- **Decision-maker**: NeurIPS / ICML reviewers
